questions.source_ref: Section 3.1: Motivation: From Normal Form to Extensive Form
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the motivation for using extensive-form games instead of only normal-form games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every finite game has an induced normal form that preserves game-theoretic properties such as Nash equilibria
Extensive form always assumes that all players act simultaneously
An extensive-form representation is in general exponentially smaller than its induced normal form
Extensive form supports solution concepts that explicitly refer to the sequence in which players act
Normal form is unusable for finite games because it cannot represent payoffs

explanations.text: The notes state that every finite game has an induced normal form preserving game-theoretic properties such as Nash equilibria.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Motivation: From Normal Form to Extensive Form
explanations.notes_quote: every finite game has an induced normal form that preserves game-theoretic properties (e.g., Nash equilibria)

explanations.text: Extensive form is useful precisely because it does not always assume simultaneous action; it can model sequential play.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Motivation: From Normal Form to Extensive Form

explanations.text: The notes explicitly compare the size of the two representations and say extensive form is generally exponentially smaller.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Motivation: From Normal Form to Extensive Form
explanations.notes_quote: is in general exponentially smaller than its induced normal form

explanations.text: This is one of the main reasons to use extensive form: it supports concepts like subgame-perfect equilibrium that depend on move order.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Motivation: From Normal Form to Extensive Form
explanations.notes_quote: Allows working with solution concepts (e.g., subgame-perfect equilibrium) that explicitly refer to the sequence in which players act

explanations.text: Normal form does represent payoffs; the issue is that it can be redundant and does not naturally encode temporal structure.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Motivation: From Normal Form to Extensive Form

questions.source_ref: Section 3.1: Applications in Artificial Intelligence and Multi-Agent Systems
questions.text: Which of the following are listed applications of extensive-form games in AI and multi-agent systems? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Game-tree search in adversarial domains such as chess and Go
Automated negotiation involving bluffing and information revelation
Sequential auctions that capture tactical bidding decisions
Security and defense settings such as patrolling and Stackelberg-style analysis
Only static one-shot voting problems with no sequential interaction

explanations.text: The notes explicitly mention classic AI problems such as computer chess and Go as sequential decision-making applications.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Applications in Artificial Intelligence and Multi-Agent Systems
explanations.notes_quote: Foundation for algorithms in classic AI problems such as computer chess and Go

explanations.text: Negotiation protocols are described as extensive-form games to model multi-stage strategic interaction, including bluffing and information revelation.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Applications in Artificial Intelligence and Multi-Agent Systems
explanations.notes_quote: Automated Negotiation... including bluffing and information revelation

explanations.text: The notes list sequential auctions as a natural extensive-form application because bids are placed in turn.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Applications in Artificial Intelligence and Multi-Agent Systems
explanations.notes_quote: Sequential auctions where bids are placed in turn are naturally described as extensive form games

explanations.text: Security and defense are explicitly included, with examples such as robot patrolling, cyber-security, and Stackelberg games.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Applications in Artificial Intelligence and Multi-Agent Systems
explanations.notes_quote: Security and Defense... often analysed via extensive form representations such as Stackelberg games

explanations.text: The notes emphasize sequential, multi-stage, and partially observable settings rather than only static one-shot problems.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Applications in Artificial Intelligence and Multi-Agent Systems

sections.title: 3.2 Extensive Form Games with Perfect Information

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe a perfect-information game in extensive form? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It is represented as a tree in which nodes correspond to choices and edges correspond to actions
Leaves represent final outcomes over which players have utility functions
Players move sequentially and know the full history of previous actions when making decisions
It necessarily contains simultaneous moves and hidden information
There is no uncertainty about past moves in perfect-information games

explanations.text: The informal definition describes the game as a tree with nodes for choices and edges for actions.
explanations.notes_quote: a tree (in the sense of graph theory) in which: Each node represents the choice of one of the players; Each edge represents a possible action

explanations.text: Terminal leaves encode the final outcomes and utilities.
explanations.notes_quote: The leaves (terminal nodes) represent final outcomes over which each player has a utility function

explanations.text: Perfect information means each player knows the full history when acting.
explanations.notes_quote: Players move sequentially, and each player, when making a decision, knows the full history of previous actions

explanations.text: Perfect-information games exclude hidden information about past moves and do not require simultaneous moves.
The notes explicitly say there is no uncertainty about past moves.
explanations.notes_quote: There is no uncertainty about past moves, no simultaneous moves, and no hidden information

questions.text: Given the tuple $$(N, A, H, Z, \chi, \rho, \sigma, u)$$ for a finite perfect-information game, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $H$ is the set of nonterminal choice nodes and $Z$ is the disjoint set of terminal nodes
$\chi : H \to 2^A$ assigns to each choice node the set of available actions
$\rho : H \to N$ assigns to each nonterminal node the player who acts there
$\sigma : H \times A \to H \cup Z$ maps a node-action pair to a successor node, and the injectivity condition ensures a tree structure
$u_i : H \to \mathbb{R}$ assigns utilities to every nonterminal node rather than to terminal nodes

explanations.text: This matches the formal definition exactly.
explanations.notes_quote: $H$ is a set of nonterminal choice nodes; $Z$ is a set of terminal nodes, disjoint from $H$

explanations.text: The action function maps each choice node to its available actions.
explanations.notes_quote: $\chi : H \to 2^A$ is the action function, which assigns to each choice node a set of possible actions

explanations.text: The player function identifies who moves at each nonterminal node.
explanations.notes_quote: $\rho : H \to N$ is the player function, which assigns to each nonterminal node a player

explanations.text: The successor function and its injectivity condition are what enforce the tree structure with no cycles.
explanations.notes_quote: $\sigma : H \times A \to H \cup Z$ is the successor function... this ensures the graph is a tree with no cycles

explanations.text: Utilities are defined on terminal nodes $Z$, not on nonterminal nodes $H$.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe histories and descendants in a perfect-information game tree? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A node can be identified with the sequence of choices leading from the root to that node
Descendants of a node are all choice and terminal nodes in the subtree rooted at that node
History is ambiguous because multiple distinct paths can lead to the same node in a tree
The tree structure makes the history of a node unambiguous
Descendants include only terminal nodes and exclude nonterminal choice nodes

explanations.text: The notes define history as the sequence of choices from the root to the node.
explanations.notes_quote: we can unambiguously identify a node with its history -- the sequence of choices leading from the root node to it

explanations.text: Descendants are defined as all nodes in the subtree rooted at the given node, including both choice and terminal nodes.
explanations.notes_quote: the descendants of a node $h$ -- all the choice and terminal nodes in the subtree rooted at $h$

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: because the structure is a tree, the history is unambiguous.
This is the reason histories can identify nodes uniquely in the tree.
explanations.notes_quote: Since choice nodes form a tree, we can unambiguously identify a node with its history

explanations.text: Descendants include both choice nodes and terminal nodes, not only leaves.

questions.text: In the Sharing Game, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Player 1 proposes one of the splits $2\text{-}0$, $1\text{-}1$, or $0\text{-}2$
After observing the proposal, Player 2 chooses whether to accept or reject
If Player 2 rejects, the payoff is $(0,0)$ regardless of the proposed split
If Player 2 accepts the split $0\text{-}2$, the payoff is $(2,0)$
The root node belongs to Player 2, who chooses yes or no before any proposal is made

explanations.text: These are exactly the three proposals available to the brother at the root.
explanations.notes_quote: The brother (Player 1) suggests a split: one of three options: He keeps both (2-0); They each keep one (1-1); She keeps both (0-2)

explanations.text: The sister's move is to accept or reject after seeing the proposal.
explanations.notes_quote: The sister (Player 2) chooses to accept or reject the proposal

explanations.text: Rejection always leads to neither sibling receiving any gift.
explanations.notes_quote: If reject on any branch: $(0, 0)$

explanations.text: Accepting $0\text{-}2$ gives the sister both presents, so the payoff is $(0,2)$, not $(2,0)$.
The root node belongs to Player 1, who first proposes the split.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.2: Pure Strategies in Perfect Information Games
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe pure strategies in perfect-information games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A pure strategy is a complete specification of deterministic actions at every node belonging to the player
The set of pure strategies of player $i$ is $$S_i = \prod_{h \in H, \rho(h) = i} \chi(h)$$
A player only needs to specify actions at nodes that are guaranteed to be reached under equilibrium play
A strategy must specify actions even at nodes that may never be reached
Pure strategies in perfect-information games are defined over information sets rather than over the player's nodes

explanations.text: The notes define a pure strategy as a complete deterministic plan for every node of that player.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Pure Strategies in Perfect Information Games
explanations.notes_quote: A pure strategy for a player in a perfect-information game is a complete specification of which deterministic action to take at every node belonging to that player

explanations.text: This is the formal definition given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Pure Strategies in Perfect Information Games
explanations.notes_quote: $$S_i = \prod_{h \in H, \rho(h) = i} \chi(h)$$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the strategy must specify actions regardless of whether the node is actually reachable.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Pure Strategies in Perfect Information Games

explanations.text: This is the key point emphasized in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Pure Strategies in Perfect Information Games
explanations.notes_quote: strategy requires a decision at each of their choice nodes, regardless of whether or not it is possible to actually reach that node

explanations.text: Strategies over information sets are used for imperfect-information games; perfect-information strategies are specified at each node.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Pure Strategies in Perfect Information Games

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the strategy sets in the Sharing Game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Player 1 has exactly 3 pure strategies: $2\text{-}0$, $1\text{-}1$, and $0\text{-}2$
Player 2 has 3 choice nodes with 2 actions each, so Player 2 has $2^3 = 8$ pure strategies
$(\text{yes,no,yes})$ is a valid pure strategy for Player 2
Player 2 has only 2 pure strategies because the only actions are yes and no
Player 1 has 6 pure strategies because each proposal can later be accepted or rejected

explanations.text: Player 1 acts only once at the root and has three available actions.
explanations.notes_quote: Player 1 has 1 choice node with 3 actions, so 3 strategies: $S_1 = \{2\text{-}0, \; 1\text{-}1, \; 0\text{-}2\}$

explanations.text: Player 2 must specify yes/no at each of three nodes, giving eight combinations.
explanations.notes_quote: Player 2 has 3 choice nodes, each with 2 actions, so $2^3 = 8$ strategies

explanations.text: This tuple appears in the listed strategy set for Player 2.
explanations.notes_quote: $S_2 = \{(\text{yes,yes,yes}), (\text{yes,yes,no}), (\text{yes,no,yes})

explanations.text: Because Player 2 has three separate decision nodes, the number of pure strategies is $2^3$, not 2.
Player 1 chooses only the proposal at the root; accept/reject choices belong to Player 2.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.2: Worked Example: Two-Player Game with Two Choice Nodes Each
questions.text: In the worked example with strategy sets $$S_1 = \{(A,G), (A,H), (B,G), (B,H)\}$$ and $$S_2 = \{(C,E), (C,F), (D,E), (D,F)\},$$ which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The induced normal form has 16 strategy profiles because each player has 4 pure strategies
The extensive form has only 5 different outcomes even though the normal form has 16 entries
Some different strategy profiles in the normal form lead to the same terminal leaf
The induced normal form removes all redundancy present in the extensive form
The profile $((B,G),(C,F))$ yields payoff $(2,10)$ in the matrix

explanations.text: With 4 strategies for each player, the normal-form matrix has $4 	imes 4 = 16$ entries.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Worked Example: Two-Player Game with Two Choice Nodes Each
explanations.notes_quote: the normal form has 16 strategy profile entries

explanations.text: The notes explicitly contrast the 5 outcomes in the tree with the 16 matrix entries.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Worked Example: Two-Player Game with Two Choice Nodes Each
explanations.notes_quote: The extensive form has only 5 different outcomes, but the normal form has 16 strategy profile entries

explanations.text: This is the source of the redundancy in the induced normal form.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Worked Example: Two-Player Game with Two Choice Nodes Each
explanations.notes_quote: Some strategy combinations lead to the same leaf

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: the normal form can contain substantial redundancy relative to the extensive form.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Worked Example: Two-Player Game with Two Choice Nodes Each

explanations.text: Reading the matrix entry in row $(B,G)$ and column $(C,F)$ gives payoff $(2,10)$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Worked Example: Two-Player Game with Two Choice Nodes Each

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the relationship between extensive form and normal form? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every perfect-information game has a corresponding normal-form representation
Temporal structure in extensive form can create redundancy when converted into normal form
Every normal-form game can be transformed into a perfect-information extensive-form game
The Prisoner's Dilemma cannot be represented as a perfect-information extensive-form game
The reverse transformation always exists because simultaneous moves can always be encoded as perfect information

explanations.text: This is the first key relationship listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Every perfect-information game has a corresponding normal form representation

explanations.text: The notes explain that temporal structure can make the induced normal form redundant.
explanations.notes_quote: the temporal structure of the extensive form can result in redundancy in the normal form

explanations.text: The reverse transformation does not always exist for perfect-information games.
The notes use the Prisoner's Dilemma as the example showing why the reverse transformation can fail.
explanations.notes_quote: The Prisoner's Dilemma cannot be represented as a perfect-information extensive form game

explanations.text: Simultaneous moves generally require imperfect information, so the reverse transformation to perfect information does not always exist.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the theorem about pure strategies in finite perfect-information games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every finite perfect-information game in extensive form has a pure strategy Nash equilibrium
The proof idea relies on backward induction
The theorem also holds unchanged for imperfect-information games such as Matching Pennies
The intuition is that when players observe the full history, randomness is not needed to find an equilibrium
This helps show that perfect-information extensive-form games form a strictly smaller class than all normal-form games

explanations.text: This theorem is stated explicitly in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Every (finite) perfect-information game in extensive form has a pure strategy Nash equilibrium

explanations.text: Backward induction is given as the proof idea.
explanations.notes_quote: Can be shown by backward induction

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the theorem and intuition do not hold for more general imperfect-information games such as Matching Pennies.
This is the intuition given for why pure strategies suffice in perfect-information games.
explanations.notes_quote: it is never necessary to introduce randomness into the action selection in order to find an equilibrium

explanations.text: The notes draw this conclusion immediately after discussing the theorem.
explanations.notes_quote: This also confirms that the set of perfect-information extensive form games is strictly smaller than the set of all normal form games

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe subgame-perfect equilibrium (SPE)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: SPE refines Nash equilibrium for dynamic games by requiring optimal behaviour after every possible history
SPE rules out non-credible threats and implausible off-path behaviour
SPE only checks rationality along the equilibrium path and ignores unreachable subgames
SPE is meaningful because extensive form records the sequence of moves
SPE is weaker than Nash equilibrium because it imposes fewer requirements

explanations.text: This is the core motivation and definition-level intuition for SPE.
explanations.notes_quote: requiring that player strategies prescribe optimal behaviour not only in the game as a whole, but after every possible history

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say SPE rules out non-credible threats.
explanations.notes_quote: It rules out non-credible threats and implausible off-path behaviour

explanations.text: SPE requires rationality in every subgame, including off-path subgames, not only along the realized path.
Because SPE refers to histories and subgames, it depends on the sequential structure encoded by extensive form.
explanations.notes_quote: after every possible history

explanations.text: SPE is stronger than Nash equilibrium because it imposes additional rationality requirements in every subgame.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.4: Nash Equilibria of the Worked Example
questions.text: For the worked example with $$S_1 = \{(A,G),(A,H),(B,G),(B,H)\}$$ and $$S_2 = \{(C,E),(C,F),(D,E),(D,F)\},$$ which of the following are Nash equilibria listed in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Exactly three Nash equilibria are listed for this game

explanations.text: This is the first Nash equilibrium listed in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Nash Equilibria of the Worked Example
explanations.notes_quote: There are three Nash equilibria: 1. $\{(A,G), (C,F)\}$

explanations.text: This is the second Nash equilibrium listed in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Nash Equilibria of the Worked Example

explanations.text: This is the third Nash equilibrium listed in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Nash Equilibria of the Worked Example

explanations.text: This profile is not listed among the three Nash equilibria in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Nash Equilibria of the Worked Example

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say there are three Nash equilibria.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Nash Equilibria of the Worked Example
explanations.notes_quote: There are three Nash equilibria

questions.text: Why is the equilibrium $\{(A,G), (C,F)\}$ subgame-perfect in the worked example? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: After $A$, Player 2 prefers $C$ to $D$ because $8 > 3$
If Player 2 plays $F$ after $B$, Player 1 prefers $G$ to $H$ because $2 > 1$
Given that continuation, Player 1 prefers $A$ to $B$ because $3 > 2$
Player 2 prefers $F$ to $E$ because $F$ leads to utility 10 while $E$ gives 5
It is subgame-perfect because some off-path actions are irrational but never reached

explanations.text: The notes compare Player 2's payoffs after $A$ and conclude $C$ is rational.
explanations.notes_quote: Player 2 at first choice node (after $A$): $C$ gives utility 8 vs. $D$ gives utility 3

explanations.text: At Player 1's later node, $G$ strictly dominates $H$ in that subtree.
explanations.notes_quote: $G$ gives utility 2 vs. $H$ gives utility 1

explanations.text: Once the continuation under $B$ is evaluated, Player 1 prefers $A$ because it yields 3 instead of 2.
explanations.notes_quote: $A$ gives utility 3 vs. $B$ gives utility 2

explanations.text: The notes explicitly justify Player 2's choice of $F$ over $E$ using the continuation payoffs.
explanations.notes_quote: $E$ gives utility 5, $F$ leads to Player 1 choosing $G$ giving utility 10. So $F$ is rational

explanations.text: SPE requires rationality in every subtree; the notes say all actions are rational in every subtree for this equilibrium.
explanations.notes_quote: All actions are rational in every subtree -- this is a subgame-perfect equilibrium

questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why $\{(B,H), (C,E)\}$ is not subgame-perfect? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: At Player 1's second choice node, $H$ gives utility 1 while $G$ gives utility 2
The strategy specifies an action that is not rational in the relevant subgame
The profile relies on a non-credible threat because the irrational action occurs off the equilibrium path
The profile is not even a Nash equilibrium of the whole game
SPE excludes such profiles because it checks every subgame, not just the whole game

explanations.text: The notes directly compare the two continuation payoffs and show $H$ is irrational there.
explanations.notes_quote: $H$ gives utility 1 while $G$ gives utility 2

explanations.text: That is exactly why the threat is non-credible: the prescribed action is not optimal in the subgame.
explanations.notes_quote: the choice of $H$ is not rational in the respective subgame

explanations.text: The notes explain that Player 1 knows this node will not be reached under the equilibrium path, so the threat is off-path and non-credible.
explanations.notes_quote: Player 1 is making a non-credible threat: they know Player 2 will choose $E$, so they will never reach this choice node

explanations.text: The notes list $\{(B,H), (C,E)\}$ as one of the Nash equilibria; the issue is that it is not subgame-perfect.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Nash Equilibria of the Worked Example

explanations.text: This is the defining refinement from NE to SPE.
explanations.notes_quote: for any subgame $G'$ of $G$, the restriction of $s$ to $G'$ is a Nash equilibrium of $G'$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the formal definition and properties of subgame-perfect equilibrium? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A subgame rooted at a nonterminal node $h$ is the restriction of the tree to the descendants of $h$
A strategy profile is an SPE if its restriction to every subgame is a Nash equilibrium of that subgame
Every SPE is also a Nash equilibrium because the whole game is itself a subgame
Every Nash equilibrium is automatically an SPE
Every perfect-information extensive-form game has at least one SPE

explanations.text: This is the definition of a subgame given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: The subgame of $G$ rooted at some nonterminal node $h$ is the restriction of the tree to the descendants of $h$

explanations.text: This is the formal definition of SPE.
explanations.notes_quote: The subgame-perfect equilibria (SPE) of a game $G$ are all strategy profiles $s$ such that for any subgame $G'$ of $G$, the restriction of $s$ to $G'$ is a Nash equilibrium of $G'$

explanations.text: Because the whole game counts as a subgame, every SPE must also be a Nash equilibrium of the whole game.
explanations.notes_quote: Every SPE is also a Nash equilibrium (since the whole game is itself a subgame)

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say not every Nash equilibrium is an SPE because some involve non-credible threats.
Existence of at least one SPE is listed as a property of perfect-information extensive-form games.
explanations.notes_quote: Every perfect-information extensive form game has at least one subgame-perfect equilibrium

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe backward induction for computing SPE? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: One starts by solving the bottom-most subgames
The equilibria of lower subgames are assumed when reasoning about larger subgames above them
The process repeats until the entire game tree is resolved
The notes state it can be implemented in time linear in the size of the game representation
Backward induction requires exponential time because every path must be recomputed from scratch

explanations.text: This is the first step in the procedure listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Identify the equilibria in the bottom-most subgame trees

explanations.text: The notes describe backing up through the tree while assuming lower equilibria will be played.
explanations.notes_quote: Assume those equilibria will be played as one backs up and considers increasingly larger trees

explanations.text: This is the final step of the procedure.
explanations.notes_quote: Repeat until the entire game tree is resolved

explanations.text: The computational complexity is explicitly stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: requiring time linear in the size of the game representation

explanations.text: The notes say the algorithm is linear-time, not exponential-time.

questions.text: In the Centipede Game example, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Backward induction predicts that Player 1 goes Down immediately at the first node
The resulting payoff under SPE is $(1,0)$
At the last node, Player 1 chooses Aside because $3 > 4$
The example illustrates that SPE can predict inefficient outcomes even when higher cooperative payoffs are available
The notes compare this inefficiency to the Prisoner's Dilemma

explanations.text: The backward-induction solution ends with immediate termination by Player 1.
explanations.notes_quote: The SPE predicts that Player 1 goes Down immediately

explanations.text: The notes give $(1,0)$ as the resulting payoff.
explanations.notes_quote: yielding payoff $(1, 0)$

explanations.text: At the last node, Player 1 chooses Down because 4 is greater than 3.
explanations.notes_quote: Last node (Player 1): D gives 4 vs. A gives 3. Choose D

explanations.text: The notes explicitly emphasize the inefficiency of the SPE prediction in this game.
explanations.notes_quote: even though both players could achieve much higher payoffs... by cooperating

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say this mirrors the inefficiency of the Prisoner's Dilemma.
explanations.notes_quote: This mirrors the inefficiency of the Prisoner's Dilemma

questions.source_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
questions.text: In the Ultimatum Game with 100 gold coins, which of the following correctly describe the normal-form representation and one Nash equilibrium? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $S_2 = \{0,1\}^{101}$ because Player 2 must specify accept/reject for each possible claim by Player 1
The strategy pair $(50, d_{50})$ is a Nash equilibrium with payoff $(50,50)$
In $d_{50}$, Player 2 accepts if and only if $i > 50$
The notes define $u_1(i,d) = i \cdot d(i)$ and $u_2(i,d) = (100-i) \cdot d(i)$

explanations.text: Player 1 can claim any integer number of coins from 0 to 100.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)

explanations.text: Player 2 must specify a response for each of the 101 possible claims.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
explanations.notes_quote: $S_2 = \{0, 1\}^{101}$ (Player 2 has $2^{101}$ pure strategies)

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify $(50, d_{50})$ as a Nash equilibrium with payoff $(50,50)$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
explanations.notes_quote: Consider the strategy pair $(50, d_{50})$... This is a Nash equilibrium

explanations.text: The notes define $d_{50}$ the other way around: accept for $i \le 50$ and reject for $i > 50$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)

explanations.text: These payoff functions are given explicitly in the normal-form description.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
explanations.notes_quote: Payoff functions: $u_1(i, d) = i \cdot d(i)$ and $u_2(i, d) = (100 - i) \cdot d(i)$

questions.source_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the subgame-perfect equilibria of the Ultimatum Game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $i \le 99$, Player 2 must accept because $100-i > 0$ is better than 0 from rejecting
If $i = 100$, both Accept and Reject are rational for Player 2 because both give payoff 0
The equilibrium $(50, d_{50})$ is subgame-perfect because rejecting 51 is rational for Player 2
The payoff of $(99, d_{99})$ is $(99,1)$ and the payoff of $(100, d_{100})$ is $(100,0)$

explanations.text: This is the subgame-by-subgame reasoning given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
explanations.notes_quote: If $i \le 99$: Player 2 gets $100 - i > 0$ from accepting vs. $0$ from rejecting. Player 2 must accept

explanations.text: At $i=100$, Player 2 gets 0 either way, so both actions are rational.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
explanations.notes_quote: If $i = 100$: Player 2 gets $0$ either way, so both $A$ and $R$ are rational

explanations.text: The notes state that there are exactly two SPE and list these two profiles.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)
explanations.notes_quote: $(99, d_{99})$ and $(100, d_{100})$ are the two subgame-perfect equilibria

explanations.text: The notes say $(50,d_{50})$ is not necessarily an SPE because rejecting at $i=51$ is not rational when accepting would give 49.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)

explanations.text: These payoffs are explicitly listed in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Worked Example: The Ultimatum Game (from LGT)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the motivation for studying repeated games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma, defection is the equilibrium in dominant strategies, yet cooperation is often observed in practice
One explanation for practical cooperation is the expectation of future interaction
Repeated interaction can support strategies that reward cooperation and punish defection
Repeated games are irrelevant to cooperation because only one-shot incentives matter
Social preferences and norms of reciprocity are also mentioned as possible reasons for cooperation

explanations.text: This is the starting puzzle motivating repeated games in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: In the Prisoner's Dilemma, defecting is the equilibrium in dominant strategies. Yet in practice, players frequently cooperate

explanations.text: Future interaction is explicitly listed as a reason cooperation may arise.
explanations.notes_quote: Expectation of future interaction

explanations.text: The notes explain that repeated play allows reward-and-punishment strategies to change incentives.
explanations.notes_quote: strategies that reward cooperation and punish defection can make mutual cooperation more attractive

explanations.text: The notes argue the opposite: repeated interaction can materially change strategic incentives.
These are the other motivations listed before future interaction.
explanations.notes_quote: Social preferences, norms of reciprocity

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the framework of repeated games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The same stage game in normal form is played multiple times by the same set of players
A key question is whether agents observe and remember earlier play
Another key question is how utility from the entire repeated interaction is defined
Repeated games require a different set of players in every round
The notes frame repeated games as a formal analysis of history dependence and overall utility

explanations.text: This is the basic definition of a repeated game in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: the same stage game (in normal form) is played multiple times by the same set of players

explanations.text: Observation and memory of earlier actions are explicitly listed as key questions.
explanations.notes_quote: Do agents see what the other agents played earlier? Do they remember?

explanations.text: The notes explicitly ask how utility from the entire repeated game should be defined.
explanations.notes_quote: What is the utility of a player from the entire repeated game?

explanations.text: The same set of players repeats the stage game; changing players each round is not part of the definition given.
This summarizes the two key formal questions highlighted in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Key questions for formal analysis

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish stationary strategies from general repeated-game strategies? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A stationary strategy is memoryless and adopts the same strategy in each stage game
In general, a player's action at a stage may depend on the history of play so far
History-dependent strategies are particularly important in infinitely repeated games
A stationary strategy must randomize differently in every round
Repeated-game strategies can never condition on past defections or cooperation

explanations.text: This is the definition of a stationary strategy given in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: A stationary strategy: adopt the same strategy in each stage game (memoryless)

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say actions can depend on the history of play.
explanations.notes_quote: the action played at a stage can depend on the history of play so far

explanations.text: The notes single out infinitely repeated games as especially important for such history dependence.
explanations.notes_quote: This is particularly important in infinitely repeated games

explanations.text: A stationary strategy is memoryless, not one that changes randomly each round.
Repeated-game strategies often do condition on past cooperation or defection; that is central to the topic.

sections.title: 3.6 Extensive Form Games with Imperfect Information

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe information sets in imperfect-information games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An imperfect-information game partitions each player's choice nodes into information sets
If two nodes are in the same information set, the player cannot distinguish between them
Nodes in the same information set may belong to different players as long as the available actions match
Nodes in the same information set must have the same available actions
Perfect-information games are the special case where every information set is a singleton

explanations.text: This is the basic structural addition that turns a perfect-information game into an imperfect-information one.
explanations.notes_quote: each player's choice nodes are partitioned into information sets

explanations.text: This is the meaning of being in the same information set.
explanations.notes_quote: If two nodes are in the same information set, the agent cannot distinguish between them

explanations.text: Nodes in the same information set must belong to the same player; the formal definition requires $\rho(h)=\rho(h')$.
The formal definition requires equal action sets within an information set.
explanations.notes_quote: nodes in the same information set must have the same available actions

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify perfect-information games as the singleton-partition special case.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Pure Strategies in Imperfect Information Games
explanations.notes_quote: Perfect-information games are a special case where every element of each partition is a singleton

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the formal definition of a finite imperfect-information game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It extends a perfect-information game tuple by adding information partitions $I = (I_1, \ldots, I_n)$
For each player $i$, $I_i$ is a partition of that player's choice nodes
If $h$ and $h'$ are in the same information set, then they must have the same acting player and the same available actions
The formal definition removes the successor function $\sigma$ because histories are no longer meaningful
The tuple is written as $$(N, A, H, Z, \chi, \rho, \sigma, u, I)$$

explanations.text: The notes define imperfect-information games by augmenting the perfect-information tuple with $I$.
explanations.notes_quote: A (finite) imperfect-information game in extensive form is a tuple $(N, A, H, Z, \chi, \rho, \sigma, u, I)$

explanations.text: Each player's information sets partition that player's decision nodes.
explanations.notes_quote: $I_i = (I_{i,1}, \ldots, I_{i,k_i})$ is a partition of $\{h \in H \mid \rho(h) = i\}$

explanations.text: This is the consistency condition imposed on nodes within the same information set.
explanations.notes_quote: nodes in the same information set must have the same available actions and the same player acting

explanations.text: The successor function remains part of the tuple; imperfect information augments rather than removes the underlying tree structure.
This is the exact tuple notation used in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: $(N, A, H, Z, \chi, \rho, \sigma, u, I)$

questions.source_ref: Section 3.6: Pure Strategies in Imperfect Information Games
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe pure strategies in imperfect-information games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A pure strategy chooses one action at each information set rather than at each node
The set of pure strategies of player $i$ is $$S_i = \prod_{I_{i,j} \in I_i} \chi(I_{i,j})$$
If two nodes are in the same information set, a player may choose different actions at those nodes because the nodes are distinct
Perfect-information games are the special case where each information set contains exactly one node
In imperfect-information games, pure strategies are undefined because players lack full history information

explanations.text: This is the key distinction from perfect-information games.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Pure Strategies in Imperfect Information Games
explanations.notes_quote: A pure strategy selects one of the available actions in each information set (not each node)

explanations.text: This is the formal strategy-set definition given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Pure Strategies in Imperfect Information Games
explanations.notes_quote: $$S_i = \prod_{I_{i,j} \in I_i} \chi(I_{i,j})$$

explanations.text: Because the player cannot distinguish nodes in the same information set, the strategy must prescribe the same action for that entire set.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Pure Strategies in Imperfect Information Games

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify singleton information sets as the perfect-information case.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Pure Strategies in Imperfect Information Games
explanations.notes_quote: Perfect-information games are a special case where every element of each partition is a singleton

explanations.text: Pure strategies are still well-defined; they are just specified over information sets rather than nodes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Pure Strategies in Imperfect Information Games

questions.text: Which of the following are key facts about imperfect-information games stated in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Perfect-information games are not expressive enough to capture the Prisoner's Dilemma and many simultaneous-move games
Any normal-form game can be trivially transformed into an equivalent imperfect-information game
Imperfect-information games may fail to admit a pure strategy Nash equilibrium
Every imperfect-information game has a pure strategy Nash equilibrium by the same theorem as perfect-information games
Imperfect-information games are strictly less expressive than normal-form games

explanations.text: This is the first key fact listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Perfect-information games were not expressive enough to capture the Prisoner's Dilemma and many other simultaneous-move games

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say any normal-form game can be transformed into an equivalent imperfect-information game.
explanations.notes_quote: any normal-form game can be trivially transformed into an equivalent imperfect-information game

explanations.text: The notes warn that pure-strategy existence can fail in imperfect-information games.
explanations.notes_quote: Imperfect-information games may not always admit a pure strategy Nash equilibrium

explanations.text: The notes explicitly deny this by contrasting imperfect-information games with the perfect-information theorem.
The notes say any normal-form game can be represented as an imperfect-information game, so they are not less expressive in that sense.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.6: Example: Prisoner's Dilemma in Extensive Form
questions.text: In the extensive-form representation of the Prisoner's Dilemma with imperfect information, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Player 1 chooses $C$ or $D$ first
Player 2's two decision nodes are in the same information set because Player 2 cannot observe Player 1's choice
The payoff $(D,d)$ is $(-3,-3)$ in the notes' payoff variant
The payoff $(C,d)$ is $(0,-4)$
This example shows how simultaneous-move structure can be represented using imperfect information

explanations.text: The notes describe Player 1 as choosing $C$ or $D$ first.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Example: Prisoner's Dilemma in Extensive Form
explanations.notes_quote: Player 1 chooses $C$ or $D$

explanations.text: This is the defining information-set feature of the example.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Example: Prisoner's Dilemma in Extensive Form
explanations.notes_quote: Player 2's two choice nodes are in the same information set (they cannot see Player 1's choice)

explanations.text: The notes list $(D,d) \to (-3,-3)$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Example: Prisoner's Dilemma in Extensive Form

explanations.text: The notes give $(C,d) \to (-4,0)$, not $(0,-4)$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Example: Prisoner's Dilemma in Extensive Form

explanations.text: The example is used to show how simultaneous-move games like the Prisoner's Dilemma require imperfect information in extensive form.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Example: Prisoner's Dilemma in Extensive Form
explanations.notes_quote: simultaneous-move games

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the finitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Within each round, players do not know what the other is playing, so there is imperfect information inside a round
After each round, players observe the outcome
The overall payoff is additive across the stage games
Backward induction implies that the only equilibrium is to always cooperate
Backward induction implies that the only equilibrium of a finitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma is to always defect

explanations.text: The notes explicitly describe imperfect information within each stage.
explanations.notes_quote: At each iteration, players do not know what the other is playing (imperfect information within a round)

explanations.text: Observation occurs after the stage outcome is realized.
explanations.notes_quote: afterwards they observe the outcome

explanations.text: The notes define the repeated-game payoff as the sum of stage-game payoffs in this finite case.
explanations.notes_quote: The payoff function is additive: the sum of payoffs in the two stage games

explanations.text: The notes say backward induction leads to always defect, not always cooperate.
This is the explicit conclusion drawn in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: the only equilibrium of a finitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma is to always defect

questions.source_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 1 -- SPE vs NE
questions.text: In SGT Exercise 1, where Player 1 chooses $U$ or $D$ and Player 2 then chooses $L$, $M$, or $R$ after $D$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The pure-strategy Nash equilibria are $(U,L)$, $(D,M)$, and $(D,R)$
$(U,L)$ is not subgame-perfect because $L$ is not a best response in the subgame after $D$
In the subgame after $D$, Player 2 should choose $M$ or $R$ because each gives payoff 1, which is better than 0 from $L$
$(D,M)$ and $(D,R)$ are ruled out as Nash equilibria because Player 1 prefers $U$ to $D$
The normal-form payoff matrix has row $U$ equal to $(3,3)$ against all three of Player 2's actions

explanations.text: These are exactly the pure-strategy Nash equilibria listed in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 1 -- SPE vs NE
explanations.notes_quote: Pure strategy Nash equilibria: $(U, L)$, $(D, M)$, and $(D, R)$

explanations.text: The notes identify $(U,L)$ as the equilibrium that fails subgame perfection.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 1 -- SPE vs NE
explanations.notes_quote: $(U, L)$ is not a subgame-perfect equilibrium, because playing $L$ is not a best response for Player 2 in the subgame

explanations.text: This is the explicit payoff comparison used in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 1 -- SPE vs NE
explanations.notes_quote: Player 2 should choose $M$ or $R$ (both give payoff 1, which is better than 0 from $L$)

explanations.text: The notes list both $(D,M)$ and $(D,R)$ as Nash equilibria, so they are not ruled out on that basis.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 1 -- SPE vs NE

explanations.text: The matrix shown in the notes has $(3,3)$ in every entry of row $U$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 1 -- SPE vs NE

questions.source_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 2 -- Card Game with Imperfect Information
questions.text: In the card game with imperfect information, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Nature deals Player 1 a High or Low card with probability 0.5 each
If Player 1 sees rather than raises, Player 1 wins the pot when the card is High and Player 2 wins when the card is Low
If Player 1 raises and Player 2 folds, the payoff is $(1,-1)$
The information set $I = \{v_3, v_4\}$ means Player 2 must make the same choice after a raise whether the hidden card is High or Low
The root node is owned by Player 2 because Player 2 decides whether the card is High or Low

explanations.text: The notes specify a fair chance move by nature.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 2 -- Card Game with Imperfect Information
explanations.notes_quote: A card is dealt to $P_1$: High (H) with probability 0.5, Low (L) with probability 0.5

explanations.text: This is exactly how the 'see' action is described.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 2 -- Card Game with Imperfect Information
explanations.notes_quote: If $P_1$ sees: $P_1$ gets pot if card is H, $P_2$ gets pot if card is L

explanations.text: The fold payoff after a raise is given explicitly.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 2 -- Card Game with Imperfect Information
explanations.notes_quote: If $P_2$ folds: $P_1$ gets the pot. Payoff: $(1, -1)$

explanations.text: Because the two nodes are in the same information set, Player 2 must choose the same action at both.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 2 -- Card Game with Imperfect Information
explanations.notes_quote: $P_2$ must make the same choice at both vertices in $I$

explanations.text: The root node is owned by nature, denoted $P_0$, not by Player 2.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.6: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 2 -- Card Game with Imperfect Information
explanations.notes_quote: The root node $v_0$ is owned by nature ("chance"), $P_0$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why infinite repetition matters in repeated games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: With a known finite horizon, backward induction still leads to defection in every round of the Prisoner's Dilemma
If the game is repeated infinitely often, or for a finite but unknown number of rounds, cooperation can emerge
Infinite repetition removes the need to define payoffs over the whole repeated interaction
The notes contrast finite known horizons with infinite or uncertain horizons
Infinite repetition guarantees cooperation regardless of incentives or discounting

explanations.text: This is the finite-horizon benchmark emphasized in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: In finitely repeated games with a known number of rounds, backward induction still leads to defection in every round

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say cooperation can emerge under infinite repetition or an unknown finite horizon.
explanations.notes_quote: when the game is repeated infinitely often (or a finite but unknown number of times), cooperation can emerge

explanations.text: Infinite repetition makes payoff definition more subtle, not less; the notes introduce average and discounted reward for this reason.
This contrast is exactly how the section motivates infinite repetition.
explanations.notes_quote: finitely repeated games with a known number of rounds... But when the game is repeated infinitely often

explanations.text: The notes require sufficient patience or a sufficiently large continuation probability; cooperation is not automatic.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe payoff definitions for infinitely repeated games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Average reward is defined as $$\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} u_i^{(j)}}{k}$$
Future discounted reward is defined as $$\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \beta^j u_i^{(j)}$$ for $0 \le \beta \le 1$
The discount factor $\beta$ can be interpreted as the probability that the game continues to the next round
The discount factor $\beta$ only has a single interpretation in the notes: pure impatience
The notes say future discounted reward gives higher weight to early payoffs than to later ones

explanations.text: This is the exact formula given for average reward.
explanations.notes_quote: $$\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} u_i^{(j)}}{k}$$

explanations.text: This is the exact discounted-reward formula in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: $$\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \beta^j u_i^{(j)}$$

explanations.text: This is one of the two interpretations of the discount factor listed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: with probability $(1 - \beta)$ the game stops at any given round (and with probability $\beta$ it continues)

explanations.text: The notes give two interpretations: time preference and continuation probability.
The notes explicitly describe discounted reward as weighting early payoffs more heavily.
explanations.notes_quote: future rewards give higher weight to early payoffs than to later ones

questions.source_ref: Section 3.7: Strategies for Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish Tit-for-Tat from Grim Trigger? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Tit-for-Tat starts by cooperating and then copies the opponent's previous action
Grim Trigger starts by cooperating and defects forever after any defection by the opponent
Tit-for-Tat has no forgiveness because one defection triggers permanent retaliation
If both players use either TFT or Grim Trigger and begin with cooperation, the notes say they cooperate forever
Both strategies are described as history-dependent rather than stationary

explanations.text: This is the definition of Tit-for-Tat given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Strategies for Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
explanations.notes_quote: starts by cooperating and thereafter chooses in round $j+1$ the very action that was chosen by their opponent in round $j$

explanations.text: This is the definition of Grim Trigger in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Strategies for Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
explanations.notes_quote: If ever the other player defects, the first player defects forever

explanations.text: Permanent retaliation is the property of Grim Trigger, not Tit-for-Tat.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Strategies for Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say both TFT and Trigger lead to perpetual cooperation when both players start cooperating.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Strategies for Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
explanations.notes_quote: If both use TFT and start with cooperation, they cooperate forever

explanations.text: Both strategies depend on past play, so they are history-dependent rather than memoryless stationary strategies.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Strategies for Infinitely Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
explanations.notes_quote: thereafter chooses... the action that was chosen by their opponent in round $j$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the equilibrium result for infinitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Tit-for-Tat and Trigger strategy are Nash equilibria for sufficiently large discount factor
The notes say these strategies can form equilibrium not only with themselves but also with each other
This shows that cooperation can be sustained in each round of an infinitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma
The result holds only for finite repeated games with a known horizon
The notes claim cooperation is impossible even with high continuation probability

explanations.text: This is the main equilibrium statement in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Both Tit-for-Tat and Trigger strategy are in Nash equilibrium for sufficiently large discount factor

explanations.text: The notes explicitly add that they can be in equilibrium with each other as well.
explanations.notes_quote: they form an equilibrium not just with themselves, but also with each other

explanations.text: This is the cooperative implication drawn from the equilibrium result.
explanations.notes_quote: This way, cooperation can be achieved in each round of an infinitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma

explanations.text: The result is about infinitely repeated games, not finite known-horizon games.
The notes say the opposite: sufficiently patient players can sustain cooperation.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: Infinitely Repeated PD with Trigger Strategy (from LGT, payoffs variant 1)
questions.text:
Given the stage game:

|   | $C$ | $D$ |
|---|-----|-----|
| $C$ | $4, 4$ | $0, 5$ |
| $D$ | $5, 0$ | $1, 1$ |

and continuation probability $p$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If both players cooperate forever, a player's expected total payoff is $$4 + 4p + 4p^2 + \ldots = \frac{4}{1-p}$$
If a player defects immediately against an opponent using trigger strategy, their expected total payoff is $$5 + p + p^2 + \ldots = 5 + \frac{p}{1-p}$$
The sustainability condition derived in the notes is $$4 + \frac{4p}{1-p} \ge 5 + \frac{p}{1-p}$$
The threshold continuation probability is $$p^* = \frac{1}{4}$$
The notes conclude cooperation is sustainable only when $$p < \frac{1}{4}$$

explanations.text: This is the geometric series for perpetual cooperation given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: Infinitely Repeated PD with Trigger Strategy (from LGT, payoffs variant 1)

explanations.text: This is the payoff stream from defecting once and then facing permanent defection.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: Infinitely Repeated PD with Trigger Strategy (from LGT, payoffs variant 1)

explanations.text: The notes derive this inequality when comparing cooperation to deviation.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: Infinitely Repeated PD with Trigger Strategy (from LGT, payoffs variant 1)

explanations.text: Solving the inequality yields the threshold stated in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: Infinitely Repeated PD with Trigger Strategy (from LGT, payoffs variant 1)

explanations.text: The notes conclude cooperation is sustainable when $p \ge \frac{1}{4}$, not when $p$ is smaller.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: Infinitely Repeated PD with Trigger Strategy (from LGT, payoffs variant 1)

questions.source_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 3 -- Repeated Game with Alternating Behaviour
questions.text:
In SGT Exercise 3 with stage game:

|   | $C$ | $D$ |
|---|-----|-----|
| $C$ | $2, 2$ | $0, 5$ |
| $D$ | $5, 0$ | $1, 1$ |

which of the following are correct according to the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The expected payoff stream for alternating $(C,C)$ in odd rounds and $(D,D)$ in even rounds is $$2 + p + 2p^2 + p^3 + 2p^4 + p^5 + \ldots$$
The continuation payoff from indefinite cooperation is $$2 + 2p + 2p^2 + \ldots = \frac{2}{1-p}$$
The deviation payoff used in the notes is $$5 + p + p^2 + \ldots = 5 + \frac{p}{1-p}$$
The threshold found in the notes is $$p^* = \frac{3}{4}$$
The notes conclude the threshold is $$p^* = \frac{1}{4}$$ for this exercise as well

explanations.text: This is the alternating payoff stream written in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 3 -- Repeated Game with Alternating Behaviour

explanations.text: The notes compute the cooperation continuation value exactly this way.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 3 -- Repeated Game with Alternating Behaviour

explanations.text: This is the deviation payoff stream used in the threshold comparison.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 3 -- Repeated Game with Alternating Behaviour

explanations.text: Solving the inequality gives the threshold stated in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 3 -- Repeated Game with Alternating Behaviour

explanations.text: The threshold $\frac{1}{4}$ belongs to the earlier repeated-PD example, not this exercise.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.7: Worked Example: SGT Exercise 3 -- Repeated Game with Alternating Behaviour

questions.source_ref: Section Key Definitions: Extensive-form and equilibrium terminology
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match key Week 3 terms with their definitions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An extensive form game is a finite tree-based representation that need not assume simultaneous action and is generally exponentially smaller than its induced normal form
A subgame is the restriction of a game tree to the descendants of a nonterminal node
A non-credible threat is an action in a strategy that is not rational in the relevant subgame
Backward induction is a procedure that solves subgames from the bottom upward and runs in linear time in the game-tree size
An induced normal form is the imperfect-information partition obtained by merging indistinguishable nodes

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for extensive form game.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Extensive-form and equilibrium terminology
explanations.notes_quote: A finite representation of a game (as a tree) that does not always assume players act simultaneously; in general exponentially smaller than its induced normal form

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for subgame.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Extensive-form and equilibrium terminology
explanations.notes_quote: Subgame | The restriction of a game tree to the descendants of a nonterminal node $h$

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for non-credible threat.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Extensive-form and equilibrium terminology
explanations.notes_quote: Non-credible threat | An action specified in a strategy that is not rational in the relevant subgame

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for backward induction.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Extensive-form and equilibrium terminology
explanations.notes_quote: Backward induction | A procedure for computing SPE by solving subgames from the bottom of the tree upward; runs in time linear in the game tree size

explanations.text: Induced normal form is the normal-form payoff representation obtained by enumerating strategy profiles, not an information partition.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Extensive-form and equilibrium terminology

questions.source_ref: Section Key Definitions: Information and repeated-game terminology
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match repeated-game and information-related terms with their definitions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An information set is a partition element of a player's choice nodes whose members are indistinguishable to that player
A repeated game is one in which the same stage game is played multiple times by the same players
A stationary strategy is a memoryless strategy that plays the same action in every repetition of the stage game
Tit-for-Tat means defect forever after the opponent defects once
Future discounted reward is defined as $$\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \beta^j u_i^{(j)}$$

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for information set.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Information and repeated-game terminology
explanations.notes_quote: Information set | A partition element of a player's choice nodes in an imperfect-information game; nodes in the same set are indistinguishable to the player

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for repeated game.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Information and repeated-game terminology
explanations.notes_quote: Repeated game | A game where the same stage game is played multiple times by the same players

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for stationary strategy.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Information and repeated-game terminology
explanations.notes_quote: Stationary strategy | A memoryless strategy that plays the same action in every repetition of the stage game

explanations.text: That is Grim Trigger, not Tit-for-Tat. Tit-for-Tat copies the opponent's previous action.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Information and repeated-game terminology

explanations.text: This matches the key-definitions table entry for future discounted reward.
explanations.section_ref: Section Key Definitions: Information and repeated-game terminology
explanations.notes_quote: Future discounted reward | Payoff in an infinitely repeated game defined as $\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \beta^j u_i^{(j)}$

questions.source_ref: Learning Objective 1: Define core concepts of non-cooperative game theory in extensive and repeated settings
questions.text: Which of the following statements integrate the core Week 3 concepts correctly? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Extensive-form games encode sequential structure explicitly, while induced normal forms enumerate complete strategy profiles
In perfect-information games, pure strategies specify actions at every player node, whereas in imperfect-information games they specify actions at every information set
Repeated games extend one-shot stage games by allowing strategies to depend on observed history and by defining utility over the entire interaction
Subgame-perfect equilibrium and backward induction are irrelevant once a game is represented as a tree
Infinite repetition can sustain cooperation under suitable continuation incentives even when one-shot play favors defection

explanations.text: This combines the notes' discussion of extensive form, temporal structure, and induced normal form.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 1: Define core concepts of non-cooperative game theory in extensive and repeated settings
explanations.notes_quote: Extensive form games represent strategic interactions as game trees... The induced normal form... derived from an extensive form game by enumerating all strategy profiles

explanations.text: This is the central distinction between pure strategies in perfect and imperfect information.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 1: Define core concepts of non-cooperative game theory in extensive and repeated settings
explanations.notes_quote: Pure strategy (perfect info)... every choice node... Pure strategy (imperfect info)... every information set

explanations.text: This integrates the repeated-game framework and strategy discussion from the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 1: Define core concepts of non-cooperative game theory in extensive and repeated settings
explanations.notes_quote: the same stage game... is played multiple times... the action played at a stage can depend on the history of play so far

explanations.text: Tree representation is exactly what makes subgame-perfect equilibrium and backward induction meaningful and useful.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 1: Define core concepts of non-cooperative game theory in extensive and repeated settings

explanations.text: This integrates the infinitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma results with the one-shot defection benchmark.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 1: Define core concepts of non-cooperative game theory in extensive and repeated settings
explanations.notes_quote: With sufficient patience... strategies like Tit-for-Tat and Grim Trigger can sustain cooperation

questions.source_ref: Learning Objective 2: Formulate and analyse strategic scenarios using extensive-form and repeated-game reasoning
questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly integrate the worked examples from Week 3? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The Sharing Game shows how a sequential proposal-and-response protocol is naturally represented as a game tree
The Centipede Game shows that backward induction can produce an inefficient SPE outcome
The Ultimatum Game shows that a Nash equilibrium need not be subgame-perfect if it relies on irrational rejection in some subgame
The finitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma shows that known finite horizons can sustain cooperation through backward induction
The infinitely repeated Prisoner's Dilemma examples show how continuation probability thresholds determine whether trigger-based cooperation is sustainable

explanations.text: This is exactly how the Sharing Game is introduced and analyzed.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 2: Formulate and analyse strategic scenarios using extensive-form and repeated-game reasoning
explanations.notes_quote: Example: The Sharing Game... Protocol... Game Tree

explanations.text: The Centipede Game is used to illustrate inefficiency under SPE via backward induction.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 2: Formulate and analyse strategic scenarios using extensive-form and repeated-game reasoning
explanations.notes_quote: The Centipede game demonstrates that SPE can predict inefficient outcomes

explanations.text: The notes explicitly use the Ultimatum Game to distinguish NE from SPE via non-rational rejection in a subgame.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 2: Formulate and analyse strategic scenarios using extensive-form and repeated-game reasoning
explanations.notes_quote: Rejecting is not rational in that subgame

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: finite known horizons lead backward induction to always defect.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 2: Formulate and analyse strategic scenarios using extensive-form and repeated-game reasoning

explanations.text: Both repeated-PD worked examples derive threshold values $p^*$ for sustaining cooperation.
explanations.section_ref: Learning Objective 2: Formulate and analyse strategic scenarios using extensive-form and repeated-game reasoning
explanations.notes_quote: The critical threshold $p^*$ is found by comparing the discounted payoff streams from cooperation vs. defection
